topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet glowed on my monitor, a stark testament to my own diligent hours. I scrolled down, my breath catching in my throat as the numbers aligned. It was the quarterly compensation breakdown, and a sudden, sickening lurch hit my stomach. I traced the figures, my hand trembling slightly against the cool plastic of the mouse. The disparity was undeniable; Sarah, the new intern who seemed to glide through the office with an almost effortless grace, was earning nearly double what I was. A cold sweat prickled my hairline. I quickly minimized the window, my heart hammering a frantic rhythm against my ribs. I felt a sudden, intense urge to check the door, to see if anyone was watching me, if they had noticed my momentary lapse in focus.

[story 2]

"Did you see the latest salary review?" Mark asked, leaning over my cubicle wall, his voice overly casual. I managed a weak nod, my gaze fixed on the beige partition separating me from him. When he mentioned Sarah’s substantial raise, a strange tightness constricted my chest. It felt like my lungs were suddenly too small to draw a full breath. I started tapping my pen rapidly against the desk, the small, sharp sounds echoing loudly in the quiet space. Every glance I stole toward Sarah’s desk was a jolt of unwelcome awareness. I focused intently on straightening a stack of papers, trying to appear completely absorbed, terrified that any movement would betray the sheer alarm churning beneath my skin.

[story 3]

I reread the pay stub three times, the ink blurring slightly under the intensity of my scrutiny. The gap between my pay and that of the junior analyst was cavernous. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the edges of my vision swim. I instinctively pulled my chair back an inch, creating a small, unnecessary distance between myself and the desk. My hands became clammy, slick with perspiration as I gripped the edge of my keyboard so tightly my knuckles turned white. I kept my eyes locked on the screen, refusing to look up, convinced that if I acknowledged the reality of the situation, everything would unravel. The silence of the office seemed amplified, pressing down on my ears.
